scenario_description: A user documents a vibrant blue owl mural on a brick wall across two visits. In the second visit, a grey construction rectangle has been painted over the center of the wall, obscuring the owl's body but leaving the head and wings visible. The user asks to identify which specific part of the original mural remains visible.

turns.text: Found this cool art in the alley today.
turns.image_description: A vertical shot of a weathered red brick wall in a narrow alleyway. Painted directly onto the bricks is a large, vibrant mural of an owl. The owl is primarily electric blue with yellow eyes and white wing tips. It is facing slightly left. The entire owl, from the top of its head down to its talons gripping a branch at the bottom, is fully visible and unobstructed. The lighting is natural daylight, casting soft shadows in the brick crevices.

turns.text: That is a striking piece! The electric blue really pops against the red bricks. Is that in the same spot as before?
Yeah, came back today but they started some work. Looks like this now.
turns.image_description: The same red brick wall in the alley. A large, solid matte grey rectangle has been painted over the center of the wall, roughly covering the area where the owl's chest, belly, and legs were located in the previous image. The grey paint is flat and featureless. Above the grey rectangle, the owl's blue head, yellow eyes, and upper crest are clearly visible. Extending outwards from the sides of the grey rectangle, the blue wings with white tips are also visible, appearing to emerge from behind the grey block. The branch at the very bottom is completely covered by the grey paint. The lighting is identical to the first image.

turns.text: Oh no, looks like the crew covered up the middle section pretty heavily. It's weird seeing the wings sticking out from the sides of that grey block.
So if I'm looking at the grey patch, what part of the original bird is actually gone? A) The head and eyes B) The wings and tail C) The body and feet D) Just the beak

ground_truth.reasoning_chain: Turn 1 establishes the full entity: a complete owl with head, wings, body, and feet. Turn 3 shows a grey rectangle obscuring the central lower portion of the wall. Visual comparison reveals the head (top) and wings (sides) are still visible above and beside the grey paint. The area covered by the grey paint corresponds exactly to the owl's torso/belly and the branch/feet seen in Turn 1. Therefore, the body and feet are the parts that are gone/obscured.
ground_truth.key_difficulty: The model must visually compare the spatial layout of the owl in Turn 1 with the obscured version in Turn 3 to determine exactly which anatomical parts fall within the boundaries of the new grey rectangle. A single-turn analysis of Turn 3 only shows 'grey paint' and 'parts of an owl', making it impossible to know what was hidden without the reference from Turn 1.

_meta.scenario: In the first visit, a user spots a vibrant blue owl mural on a brick wall in an alley. In the second visit, a construction crew has painted a large grey rectangle over the center of the wall, obscuring the owl's body but leaving its head and wings visible. The user asks to identify which part of the original owl remains.
